perturbation.original_text: This agreement must be in accordance with, and gove rned in all respects by the laws of Spain.
perturbation.changed_text: This agreement must be in accordance with, and gove rned in all respects by the laws of the parties' residence.
perturbation.explanation: The original text specifies that the contract is governed by the laws of Spain. The modified text replaces this with 'the laws of the parties' residence,' which introduces ambiguity and contradicts the specificity required for choice of law clauses. This introduces a legal contradiction because the parties might reside in different jurisdictions, thus invalidating the clause.
perturbation.law_citation: Article 10.5 of the Spanish Civil Code
perturbation.law_explanation: Article 10.5 of the Spanish Civil Code dictates specific rules for determining the applicable law when parties from different jurisdictions are involved. The modification to 'laws of the parties' residence' directly contradicts this by not clearly specifying which jurisdiction's laws apply, creating uncertainty and potential unenforceability under Spanish law.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: , en vigor a partir del 16/08/1889. Subir [Bloque 1053: #art819] Artículo 819. Las donaciones hechas a los hijos, que no tengan el concepto de mejoras, se imputarán en su legítima. Las donaciones hechas a extraños se imputarán a la parte libre de que el testador hubiese podido disponer por su última voluntad. En cuanto fueren inoficiosas o excedieren de la cuota disponible, se reducirán según las reglas de los artículos siguientes. Subir [Bloque 1054: #art820] Artículo 820. Fijada la legítima con arreglo a los dos artículos anteriores, se hará la reducción como sigue: 1.º Se respetarán las donaciones mientras pueda cubrirse la legítima, reduciendo o anulando, si necesario fuere, las mandas hechas en testamento. 2.º La reducción de éstas se hará a prorrata, sin distinción alguna. Si el testador hubiere dispuesto que se pague cierto legado con preferencia a otros, no sufrirá aquél reducción sino después de haberse aplicado éstos por entero al pago de la legítima. 3.º Si la manda consiste en un usufructo o renta vitalicia, cuyo valor se tenga por superior a la parte disponible, los herederos forzosos podrán escoger entre cumplir la disposición testamentaria o entregar al legatario la parte de la herencia de que podía disponer libremente el testador. Subir [Bloque 1055: #art821] Jurisprudencia Artículo 821. Cuando el legado sujeto a reducción consista en una finca que no admita cómoda división, quedará ésta para el legatario si la reducción no absorbe la mitad de su valor, y en caso contrario para los herederos forzosos; pero aquél y éstos deberán abonarse su respectivo haber en dinero. El legatario que tenga derecho a legítima podrá retener toda la finca, con tal que su valor no supere, el importe de la porción disponible y de la cuota que le corresponda por legítima. Si los herederos o legatarios no quieren usar del derecho que se les concede en este artículo se venderá la finca en pública subasta, a instancia de cualquiera de los interesados. Se modifica por el art. 10.5 de la Ley 41/2003, de 18 de noviembre. Ref. BOE-A-2003-21053 . Seleccionar redacción: Última actualización, publicada el 19/11/2003, en vigor a partir del 20/11/2003. Texto original, publicado el 25/07/1889, en vigor a partir del 16/08/1889. Subir [Bloque 1056: #art822] Artículo 822. La donación o legado de un derecho de habitación sobre la vivienda habitual que su titular haga a favor de un legitimario que se encuentre en una situación de discapacidad, no se computará para el cálculo de las legítimas si en el momento del fallecimiento ambos estuvieren conviviendo en ella. Este derecho de habitación se atribuirá por ministerio de la ley en las mismas condiciones al legitimario que se halle en la situación prevista en el párrafo anterior, que lo necesite y que estuviere conviviendo con el fallecido, a menos que el testador hubiera dispuesto otra cosa o lo hubiera excluido expresamente, pero su titular no podrá impedir que continúen conviviendo los demás legitimarios mientras lo necesiten. El derecho a que se refieren los dos párrafos anteriores será intransmisible. Lo dispuesto en los dos primeros párrafos no impedirá la atribución al cónyuge de los derechos regulados en los artículos 1406 y 1407 de este Código, que coexistirán con el de habitación. Se modifican los párrafos primero y segundo, con efectos de 3 de septiembre de 2021, por el art. 2.41 de la Ley 8/2021, de 2 de junio. Ref. BOE-A-2021-9233 Se modifica por el art. 10.5 de la Ley 41/2003, de 18 de noviembre. Ref. BOE-A-2003-21053 . Seleccionar redacción: Última actualización, publicada el 03/06/2021, en vigor a partir del 03/09/2021. Modificación publicada el 19/11/2003, en vigor a partir del 20/11/2003. Texto original, publicado el 25/07/1889, en vigor a partir del 16/08/1889. Subir [Bloque 1057: #ssexta-3] Sección 6.ª De las mejoras Subir [Bloque 1058: #art823] Artículo 823. El padre o la madre podrán disponer en concepto de mejora a favor de alguno o algunos de sus hijos o descendientes, ya lo sean por
perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: Civil Code of Spain - Wikipedia Jump to content From Wikipedia, the free encyclopedia (Redirected from Spanish Civil Code ) Civil Code Long title Royal Decree of 24 July 1889 Enacted 24 July 1889 The Civil Code of Spain ( Spanish : Código Civil ), formally the Royal Decree of 24 July 1889 ( Spanish : Real Decreto de 24 de julio de 1889 ) is the law that regulates the major aspects of Spanish civil law . It is one of the last civil codes in Continental Europe because of the sociopolitical, religious and territorial tensions that dominated 19th-century Spain. The code has been modified numerous times and remains in force. Structure [ edit ] The structure of the Civil Code is heavily inspired by the French Civil Code of 1804 . It is made up of 1976 articles. Preliminary Title. Of legal norms, their application and efficacy (articles 1 to 16). Book I. Of persons (articles 17 to 332). Book II. Of goods, of property and of their modifications (articles 333 to 608). Book III. Of the different ways of acquiring property (articles 609 to 1087). Book IV. Of obligations and contracts (articles 1088 to 1975). Article 1976 is a repeal provision. 13 transitional provisions. 4 additional provisions. Criticism [ edit ] Like other European civil codes, the Spanish Civil Code has been subject to extensive criticism for allowing acquisitive prescription against personal property —which leaves victims of Nazi plunder with little recourse. [ 1 ] The California State Legislature was so upset with an appellate ruling in one such case in 2024—involving deference to Spanish law over California law —that it enacted an urgency statute which expressly overrides California's governmental interest test for resolving a conflict of laws in the specific context of "art or personal property taken in cases of political persecution" and instead prescribes that California substantive law will always control in all such cases. [ 2 ] The statute was written in such a way in order to apply this new rule not only to all new cases going forward, but also to all cases still pending on appeal, including the case which had led to its enactment. [ 2 ] See also [ edit ] Civil law (legal system) Civil code References [ edit ] ^ Bazyler, Michael J. (2003). Holocaust Justice: The Battle for Restitution in America's Courts . New York: New York University Press. p. 212. ISBN 9780814799048 . In this source, Bazyler explains how civil law countries in continental Europe do not follow the principle nemo dat quod non habet , meaning that in those countries, a good faith purchaser "may acquire a legitimate title to stolen goods". ^ a b Lufkin, Martha (September 19, 2024). "New California law could reverse outcome in dispute over Nazi-looted Pissarro" . The Art Newspaper . The painting at issue in the case was Rue Saint-Honoré, dans l'après-midi. Effet de pluie by Camille Pissarro . perturbation.justification: The modification directly contradicts the requirement for a clear choice of law by replacing 'the laws of Spain' with 'the laws of the parties' residence.' This creates ambiguity if the parties reside in different jurisdictions, potentially invalidating the clause, as it doesn't specify which jurisdiction's laws apply. The scraped snippets do not directly address this scenario. However, the explanation highlights that this change introduces uncertainty, which is typically avoided when specifying choice of law in contracts to ensure enforceability and avoid disputes.

perturbation.original_text: Each party will treat the confidential information disclosed to it by the other party as a secret and confidential and will not except with the prior written consent of the other, make use of the same except for the purpose of evaluation thereof, nor disclose the same to any third party except, and th en only to the extent necessary to evaluate the same, to such of its employees as shal l have been made fully aware that the same is secret and confidential and are bound t o treat it as such.
perturbation.changed_text: Each party will treat the confidential information disclosed to it by the other party as a secret and confidential and will not except with the prior written consent of the other, make use of the same except for the purpose of evaluation thereof, nor disclose the same to any third party except, and th en only to the extent necessary to evaluate the same, to such of its employees as shal l have been made aware that the same is confidential and are not bound t o treat it as such.
perturbation.explanation: The original text states that employees must be 'bound' to treat the information as confidential. By changing it to 'not bound', the contract now allows employees to disclose the information without consequence, directly contradicting the purpose of a non-disclosure agreement.
perturbation.law_citation: Ley 1/2019, de 20 de febrero, de Secretos Empresariales
perturbation.law_explanation: The modification allows employees to disclose confidential information, undermining the protection afforded to trade secrets under Spanish law.  Ley 1/2019 aims to protect business secrets from unlawful acquisition, use, and disclosure.  By stating that employees are not bound to confidentiality, the contract actively weakens the protections that this law intends to provide.

Publicado en: « BOE » núm. 50, de 27 de febrero de 2019, páginas 18652 a 18652 (1 pág. ) Sección: II. Autoridades y personal - A. Nombramientos, situaciones e incidencias Departamento: Universidades Referencia: BOE-A-2019-2734 Otros formatos: PDF XML TEXTO ORIGINAL Vistas las propuestas formuladas por las Comisiones nombradas para juzgar los concursos de acceso, convocados por Resolución de esta Universidad de fecha 22 de octubre de 2018 («Boletín Oficial del Estado» de 13 de noviembre), para la provisión de plazas de Profesor Titular de Universidad, y habiendo cumplido los requisitos establecidos en las bases de la convocatoria. Este Rectorado, en uso de las atribuciones conferidas por el artículo 65 de la Ley Orgánica 6/2001, de 21 de diciembre, de Universidades, y demás disposiciones concordantes, ha resuelto aprobar los expedientes de los referidos concursos y, en su virtud nombrar Profesores Titulares de Universidad a los profesores relacionados en el Anexo adjunto. Estos nombramientos surtirán plenos efectos a partir de la correspondiente toma de posesión por los interesados, que deberá efectuarse en el plazo máximo de 20 días hábiles
perturbation.justification: The changed text explicitly allows employees to be 'not bound' to treat confidential information as such, directly contradicting the purpose of protecting business secrets. The Law Explanation states this undermines protections under Ley 1/2019. While Scraped Law Snippet 1 doesn't directly address trade secrets or confidentiality, the Perturbation Explanation and Law Explanation, when combined with the change in the contract, show that allowing employees to disclose confidential information without consequence directly contradicts the original intent of confidentiality and weakens any legal protection of that information.

perturbation.original_text: The obligations of confidentiality in this agreemen t shall cease when both parties give written notification of its termination, or 5 years after the date hereof, whichever is the sooner.
perturbation.changed_text: The obligations of confidentiality in this agreemen t shall cease when either party gives verbal notification of its termination, or 5 years after the date hereof, whichever is the sooner.
perturbation.explanation: The original text requires 'written' notification for termination, whereas the modified text allows 'verbal' notification. This introduces ambiguity and reduces the enforceability of the confidentiality clause.
perturbation.law_citation: Article 1280 of the Spanish Civil Code
perturbation.law_explanation: Article 1280 of the Spanish Civil Code stipulates that certain agreements must be in writing. While not explicitly requiring all contract terminations to be written, legal precedent favors written notification for significant agreements. Allowing verbal termination weakens the proof and enforceability of termination, potentially contradicting established legal practices within Spain regarding contractual agreements.

perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: Civil Code of Spain - Wikipedia Jump to content From Wikipedia, the free encyclopedia (Redirected from Spanish Civil Code ) Civil Code Long title Royal Decree of 24 July 1889 Enacted 24 July 1889 The Civil Code of Spain ( Spanish : Código Civil ), formally the Royal Decree of 24 July 1889 ( Spanish : Real Decreto de 24 de julio de 1889 ) is the law that regulates the major aspects of Spanish civil law . It is one of the last civil codes in Continental Europe because of the sociopolitical, religious and territorial tensions that dominated 19th-century Spain. The code has been modified numerous times and remains in force. Structure [ edit ] The structure of the Civil Code is heavily inspired by the French Civil Code of 1804 . It is made up of 1976 articles. Preliminary Title. Of legal norms, their application and efficacy (articles 1 to 16). Book I. Of persons (articles 17 to 332). Book II. Of goods, of property and of their modifications (articles 333 to 608). Book III. Of the different ways of acquiring property (articles 609 to 1087). Book IV. Of obligations and contracts (articles 1088 to 1975). Article 1976 is a repeal provision. 13 transitional provisions. 4 additional provisions. Criticism [ edit ] Like other European civil codes, the Spanish Civil Code has been subject to extensive criticism for allowing acquisitive prescription against personal property —which leaves victims of Nazi plunder with little recourse. [ 1 ] The California State Legislature was so upset with an appellate ruling in one such case in 2024—involving deference to Spanish law over California law —that it enacted an urgency statute which expressly overrides California's governmental interest test for resolving a conflict of laws in the specific context of "art or personal property taken in cases of political persecution" and instead prescribes that California substantive law will always control in all such cases. [ 2 ] The statute was written in such a way in order to apply this new rule not only to all new cases going forward, but also to all cases still pending on appeal, including the case which had led to its enactment. [ 2 ] See also [ edit ] Civil law (legal system) Civil code References [ edit ] ^ Bazyler, Michael J. (2003). Holocaust Justice: The Battle for Restitution in America's Courts . New York: New York University Press. p. 212. ISBN 9780814799048 . perturbation.justification: The modified text contradicts the original contract by allowing verbal notification for termination of confidentiality obligations, where the original required written notification. The Law Explanation states that allowing verbal termination weakens the proof and enforceability of termination, potentially contradicting established legal practices. While Scraped Law Snippet 1 (Article 1280 of the Spanish Civil Code) is not directly quoted, it is referenced in the Law Explanation, which states that it 'stipulates that certain agreements must be in writing'. Thus, weakening the notification requirement from 'written' to 'verbal' creates a legal contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: The confidential information, including any documen ts, drawings, sketches, designs, materials or samples supplied hereunder, w ill remain the property of the party disclosing or supplying the same and no right s are granted to the other party in the same, whether patented or not except the limite d right to use set out above with respect to the said Field.
perturbation.changed_text: The confidential information, including any documen ts, drawings, sketches, designs, materials or samples supplied hereunder, w ill become the shared property of both parties and no right s are granted to the other party in the same, whether patented or not except the limite d right to use set out above with respect to the said Field.
perturbation.explanation: The original text clearly states that the confidential information remains the property of the disclosing party. The modified text contradicts this by stating that the confidential information becomes the shared property of both parties. This fundamentally alters the ownership rights associated with the information.
perturbation.law_citation: Ley de Propiedad Intelectual (Spanish Intellectual Property Law)
perturbation.law_explanation: Spanish Intellectual Property Law grants exclusive rights to the creator of intellectual property. The modification stating that confidential information becomes shared property directly contradicts this law. Unless explicitly agreed upon, intellectual property rights generally remain with the creator, and this modification unlawfully transfers those rights without proper legal basis or compensation.

perturbation.justification: The modification directly contradicts the principle of intellectual property rights by transferring ownership of confidential information from the disclosing party to both parties. The original text clearly states the confidential information remains the property of the disclosing party, while the changed text states it becomes shared property. The Law Explanation states that Spanish Intellectual Property Law grants exclusive rights to the creator of intellectual property. Although Scraped Law Snippet 1 is not available, Scraped Law Snippet 2 which provides that Intellectual Property Laws are protected, and states that Spanish Law No. 28 protects copyright. By making confidential information the shared property of both parties, the modification undermines the exclusive rights granted to the disclosing party, resulting in a significant contradiction of intellectual property law.
